name: Common Lisp
summary: ANSI-standardised Lisp with the richest export-list mechanism in the survey: defpackage declares a package's :use, :nicknames, :shadow, :shadowing-import-from, :import-from, :export, :intern relationships in one form; namespaces symbols (not bindings); ASDF provides system-level distribution above packages.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Common Lisp (ANSI X3.226-1994) is a multi-paradigm Lisp dialect with the richest export-list mechanism among mainstream languages. Production implementations: SBCL (canonical optimising compiler — `data/sbcl`), CCL, ECL, ABCL (JVM), Allegro CL, LispWorks. This record covers the language-level module-system surface; image-based development and incremental redefinition live in `data/lisp-incremental-redefinition`.

sections.title: defpackage and symbol namespacing
sections.content:
A package declaration looks like:

```lisp
(defpackage #:my-app
  (:use #:cl)
  (:nicknames #:app)
  (:shadow #:format)
  (:shadowing-import-from #:alexandria #:hash-table-keys)
  (:import-from #:bordeaux-threads #:make-thread)
  (:export #:run #:configure)
  (:intern #:helper))
```

The distinguishing property of Common Lisp packages: **they namespace symbols, not bindings**. A symbol — the canonical name token — is *interned* in exactly one home package, but can be made *accessible* in many other packages via `use`, `import`, or qualified reference. Functions, variables, classes, and macros all live in symbols, so namespacing-by-symbol governs all of them uniformly.

Six distinct relationships exist: present-and-internal (interned in the package, not exported), present-and-external (interned and exported), inherited (visible via a `:use`d package), shadowed (a local symbol blocks an inherited one of the same name), shadowing-imported (an imported symbol blocks an inherited one), and uninterned (`#:foo` syntax — a symbol belonging to no package).

sections.title: Qualified syntax and in-package
sections.content:
Qualified syntax: `pkg::name` accesses an internal symbol (double-colon, by convention discouraged in client code); `pkg:name` accesses an exported symbol (single-colon, the public-API form). The lexical convention itself encodes the visibility distinction. `IN-PACKAGE` switches the current package for subsequent reads.

This is one of the cleanest production examples of **lexical syntax encoding visibility distinctions** without language-level visibility keywords. The double-colon is harder to type and read, which discourages reaching into another package's internals; the single-colon is the natural form for public API.

sections.title: ASDF and Quicklisp — system-level distribution
sections.content:
**ASDF (Another System Definition Facility)** is the system-definition layer that sits above packages — the equivalent of Cargo or npm. An ASDF system declared in a `.asd` file lists components (Lisp files) with dependencies, supports versions, and integrates with **Quicklisp** (the de facto package archive) for distribution.

The two-layer split is deliberate: packages are language-level symbol-namespace machinery; ASDF systems are ecosystem-level distribution units. They can be one-to-one but commonly aren't — a single ASDF system can define multiple packages.

The lesson: **a sufficiently rich symbol-namespace operation set (use, import, shadowing-import, intern, export, shadow) eliminates the need for separate "import keyword variants"**. Languages like Python and Java have one `import` keyword and accumulate workarounds for partial imports, renames, and conflicts; Common Lisp expresses all those operations as orthogonal options on `defpackage`. The cost is conceptual surface area — `:shadow` vs `:shadowing-import-from` vs `:import-from` is a non-obvious distinction.
